title: OT/ICS Threat Intel - Marine
description: Curated OT/ICS cybersecurity advisories, incidents and threat intelligence from primary sources. Editorial summaries by Mike Holcomb in partnership with UtilSec.

_otthreatfeed.usage: Free to reuse with attribution. Credit 'Mike Holcomb & UtilSec' and link back to otthreatfeed.com and to each item's linked primary source. Summaries are editorial paraphrase - verify against the primary source before acting.

items.title: Max-severity SimpleHelp auth-bypass stays actively exploited past its federal KEV deadline — the remote-support tool vendors use to reach vessels and terminals
items.content_text: CISA added CVE-2026-48558, a CVSS 10.0 authentication-bypass in the SimpleHelp remote monitoring and management (RMM) platform, to its KEV catalog on 29 June; the 2 July federal deadline lapsed three days ago and exploitation continues. On OIDC-configured servers SimpleHelp fails to verify identity-token signatures, so an unauthenticated attacker can forge a fully authenticated “Technician” session — administrative control of the remote-access server, in some configurations bypassing MFA — and attackers are already dropping the TaskWeaver loader and Djinn infostealer through it. Roughly 14,000 servers are online, an estimated 1,000 directly vulnerable. RMM platforms are how engine OEMs, automation vendors and terminal integrators reach shipboard and port systems — the trusted-vendor pathway CYTUR's reporting (below) flags as the sector's highest-impact vector. Patch immediately, pull consoles off the internet, rotate technician credentials and OIDC secrets, and hunt connected endpoints; U.S.-flag operators must report confirmed incidents to the National Response Center under the USCG cyber rule.

items.title: GPS spoofing at industrial scale — on the order of 1,000 disruption incidents a day touching tens of thousands of vessels
items.content_text: Maritime cyber reporting describes GNSS interference operating at industrial scale: roughly 1,000 spoofing/disruption incidents per day affecting more than 40,000 vessels, concentrated around conflict regions but bleeding into major trade lanes. The precedents operators cite are concrete — the container ship MSC Antonia ran aground in May 2025 after spoofed data corrupted its navigation picture, and over 3,000 vessels were disrupted in the Persian Gulf in under two weeks in June 2025 — and the interference environment has not improved since. Bridge teams should treat GNSS as a degradable input, not ground truth: train watchkeepers to cross-check position against radar, visual and inertial references, configure alerts for implausible position or time jumps, log and report interference events (U.S.-flag operators: to the National Response Center, consistent with USCG incident-reporting rules), and rehearse navigating through a spoofed approach in drills.

items.title: Anubis ransomware attack on the Adriatic Port Authority (Port of Ancona) dissected — an all-IT compromise that still rerouted vessels
items.content_text: Resecurity's analysis (published 11 June) examines the Anubis ransomware group's attack on the Adriatic Port Authority, which runs Italy's Port of Ancona. The details underscore how confirmed and claimed accounts can diverge: the authority itself dates the breach to 11 December 2025, says Anubis claimed it and leaked data in January 2026, and puts the loss at roughly 2% of its data with backups preserving the rest — though employee records did reach the dark web. Resecurity's account goes further, describing crippled operations, rerouted vessels, exfiltrated contracts, employee records and port safety plans, and a reported $10 million Bitcoin demand. The instructive point for defenders: Resecurity assesses the attack never touched operational technology, working purely through IT weaknesses — a spear-phish into the managing company, then insecure Office 365/Azure cloud accounts — yet taking down the port-community and terminal layer was enough to disrupt operations. Port authorities and terminal operators should verify offline, tested backups for PCS/TOS and customs-interface systems, enforce MFA on cloud/identity accounts, segment those platforms from OT, rehearse manual/degraded gate and berth operations, and monitor leak sites for their own data.

_otthreatfeed.date_note: Breach Dec 2025 — Resecurity analysis 11 Jun, reported 15 Jun 2026

items.title: CYTUR's 2026 Maritime Cyber Threat White Paper: incidents up 103% — and ransomware is now reaching shipboard OT
items.content_text: CYTUR's 2026 Maritime Cyber Threat White Paper reports that maritime cyber incidents jumped 103% in 2025 over the prior year, dominated by DDoS, ransomware and malware infections whose growth rate more than doubled. Two findings matter most for operators. First, ransomware that once stopped at shipping-company IT is now directly reaching shipboard OT — including ballast-water control and engine monitoring — increasingly causing operational shutdowns rather than just office outages. Second, CYTUR flags compromised software-update servers and communication service providers as the highest-impact attack vector: one implant in a trusted update pathway can reach thousands of vessels simultaneously, a risk that grows as autonomous navigation and remote maintenance spread. The report also frames 2026 as the “first year of practical verification” for IACS UR E26/E27, as newbuilds contracted after the rules took effect in July 2024 begin delivering in earnest. Inventory the update pathways feeding your bridge and engine-room systems, demand E26/E27 evidence from yards and system vendors, and segment crew/business IT hard away from shipboard OT.

items.title: Dragos Q1 2026 ransomware analysis: 1,020 industrial victims — and IT outages keep cascading into OT downtime
items.content_text: Dragos' industrial ransomware analysis for the first quarter of 2026 counted 1,020 ransomware incidents impacting industrial organizations worldwide, holding at the elevated baseline established in late 2025. Manufacturing again absorbed the largest share — 633 victims, 62% of all observed industrial ransomware victims — with construction-related manufacturing, industrial-equipment producers and food & beverage makers hit hardest. Qilin and Akira generated the highest claim volumes, with The Gentleman, LockBit5.0 and Play rounding out the top five operations. Notably, Dragos observed no ransomware variants engineered to manipulate industrial control protocols; the damage mechanism remains indirect but reliable — loss of ERP platforms, virtualization infrastructure, identity services and remote-access gateways cascading into OT disruption, safety risk and extended downtime. Maritime and logistics operators appeared throughout the quarter's casework: port, shipping and logistics victims demonstrate how enterprise-side ransomware translates into berth scheduling, gate operations and cargo-flow disruption without ever touching a vessel's OT.

items.title: CISA flags auth-bypass and CSRF flaws in ST Engineering iDirect satellite terminals — the VSAT modems connecting vessels and remote sites
items.content_text: CISA published advisory ICSA-26-183-01 on 2 July covering ST Engineering iDirect Evolution iQ-Series, 3315-Series and 9-Series satellite terminals running software 4.5.2.1 and earlier — equipment deployed worldwide across transportation, energy and communications, and a staple of maritime VSAT connectivity. The advisory describes two issues, reported with CVSS v3.1 scores of 8.6 and 8.1: REST API endpoints (including /api/identity) that respond without authentication, letting anyone with network access pull the terminal's serial number, Device ID, Terminal Private Key identifier, MAC address and exact firmware version — identifiers used for satellite-network authentication, raising terminal-impersonation and reconnaissance risk — and a cross-site request forgery weakness in which state-changing endpoints such as /api/reboot accept requests authenticated only by a session cookie, so a lured browser session can reboot the terminal and drop the vessel's connectivity. No public exploitation has been reported to CISA. For ships, the VSAT terminal is the WAN link everything rides on — including remote-maintenance paths into bridge and engine-room systems. Update terminals to 4.5.2.2 or newer at the next connectivity window, keep terminal management interfaces off crew and business LANs, and confirm shoreside management portals are not internet-exposed.

items.title: Progress Kemp LoadMaster pre-auth RCE now seeing exploitation attempts — the load balancer that fronts enterprise and OT-facing services
items.content_text: eSentire's Threat Response Unit reported that CVE-2026-8037 (CVSS 9.8), a pre-authentication OS command-injection flaw in Progress Kemp LoadMaster, began drawing active exploitation attempts on 29 June — days after a public proof-of-concept and detailed write-up landed. The bug sits in the appliance's API: unsanitized input to the “/accessv2” endpoint lets an unauthenticated attacker inject commands and run code as root. The same defect affects other Progress ADC products (ECS Connection Manager, ObjectScale Connection Manager and the MOVEit WAF). eSentire noted the attempts it saw failed and produced no post-compromise activity, but with a working PoC circulating, successful exploitation is expected to follow. LoadMaster and ADC gear commonly sits at the network edge in front of web, VPN, remote-access and port-community services at terminals and shipping companies — a pre-auth root takeover there is a strong pivot point toward operational systems. Apply Progress's fixed release now, restrict management/API access, and pull the appliance off any untrusted network.

_otthreatfeed.date_note: Exploitation attempts from 29 Jun 2026 — reported 1 Jul 2026

items.title: New “CitrixBleed-pattern” NetScaler flaw exploited within 24 hours of disclosure — memory leakage from remote-access gateways
items.content_text: Citrix shipped fixes this week for six NetScaler ADC / NetScaler Gateway vulnerabilities, and the headline one — CVE-2026-8451 (CVSS 8.8), an insufficient-input-validation bug that causes a memory overread in the appliance's SAML handling — was being exploited in the wild less than 24 hours after public disclosure. Researchers at watchTowr showed that a malformed SAML authentication request padded with whitespace forces the parser to read past its buffer and return adjacent process memory to the client, echoing the CitrixBleed flaws whose leaked session tokens fueled major intrusions in 2023–24; honeypot operators observed live exploitation attempts beginning 30 June. Exposure requires the appliance to be configured as a SAML identity provider, which narrows the affected population but does not diminish the pattern. NetScaler gateways front VPN and remote-access services at shipping companies, terminals and port authorities that bridge toward operational systems, and leaked memory can include material that lets attackers hijack authenticated sessions. Apply the fixed builds now, confirm whether any NetScaler is configured for SAML, terminate active sessions and rotate credentials after patching, and watch authentication logs for anomalous SAML requests.

_otthreatfeed.date_note: Patched late Jun 2026 — exploitation from 30 Jun, reported 1–2 Jul 2026

items.title: The Coast Guard's maritime cyber rule is mid-phase-in — incident reporting and training live now, approved cyber plans due July 2027
items.content_text: The U.S. Coast Guard's “Cybersecurity in the Marine Transportation System” final rule is now well into its phased rollout: mandatory reporting of cyber incidents to the National Response Center has applied to covered U.S.-flagged vessels, facilities and OCS facilities since 16 July 2025, cybersecurity training requirements took hold on 12 January 2026 (with annual refreshers), and every covered vessel and facility must submit a Coast Guard-approved cybersecurity plan — supported by a completed cybersecurity assessment — no later than 16 July 2027. That plan deadline is now roughly a year out, which in practice means assessments, gap remediation and plan drafting belong in this year's budget and yard-period planning, not next year's. The Coast Guard has noted it is accepting plan submissions but is still finalizing its review-and-approval procedures. Operators should confirm incident-reporting procedures actually reach the NRC, evidence their training completion, and start assessments early enough that findings can be fixed before the plan goes to the Coast Guard.

_otthreatfeed.date_note: Phase-in ongoing — plans & assessments due 16 Jul 2027
